title: Which Dictionary Should I Use?
description: A scenario-based quiz on choosing the right CDSL dictionary for a task — reading classical vs Buddhist vs Vedic Sanskrit, composing Sanskrit, finding synonyms, identifying plants/names, and more. Grounded in the per-dictionary guide pages.

source.note: Every answer is grounded in this guide's featured dictionary pages (docs/dictionaries/*.mdx), each verified against the dictionary's own source. See the catalog for all 42.

questions.question: You want the broadest classical Sanskrit→English reference, one that also marks Vedic accents. Which dictionary?
questions.explanation: MW is the standard general reference — encyclopaedic, rich in compounds and citations, and it marks Vedic accents.

questions.question: You're reading the Mahāvastu and a word — looking like a Sanskritized Pali form — isn't in Monier-Williams. Where next?
questions.explanation: BHS is the specialist dictionary of Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit; it indexes the Buddhist canon and triangulates each form against Pali and classical Sanskrit.

questions.question: You want the deepest scholarly treatment with the fullest citations, and German is fine. Which?
questions.explanation: The Großes Petersburger Wörterbuch (PWG) is the deepest scholarly Sanskrit dictionary; PW is its shorter version, SCH supplements it, CCS distils it.

questions.question: You want clean, numbered senses and translated literary quotations for classical (non-Vedic) reading. Which?
questions.explanation: Apte is the classical reader's companion — numbered senses, idioms, and quotations with translations. AP (1957–59) is the larger revised edition.

questions.question: You have an English word and need its Sanskrit equivalents — i.e. you're composing Sanskrit. Which?
questions.explanation: AE (and Borooah, BOR) are English→Sanskrit dictionaries for composition — the reverse direction of the reading dictionaries.

questions.question: You want a modern French gloss. Which dictionary?
questions.explanation: STC (1932) is the standard French dictionary; Burnouf (BUR, 1866) is the older French one it superseded.

questions.question: You want Latin glosses and Indo-European cognates by the founder of comparative grammar. Which?
questions.explanation: Bopp's Glossarium Sanscritum (1847) glosses in Latin and cites cognates (e.g. Latin axilla for aṃśa).

questions.question: You need to identify a Sanskrit plant name botanically. Which resource?
questions.explanation: Meulenbeld's SNP maps Sanskrit plant names to their botanical identity with a thorough source apparatus.

questions.question: You meet an administrative/fiscal term in an inscription and need its technical sense. Which?
questions.explanation: Sircar's IEG glosses the technical terms of inscriptions; PGN indexes the personal/place names in Gupta inscriptions.

questions.question: You want to identify and locate a proper name in the Mahābhārata. Which?
questions.explanation: Sörensen's INM is the name-index to the epic; the Mahābhārata Cultural Index (MCI) adds cultural detail.

questions.question: You want the story and identity behind a Purāṇic name, in clear English. Which?
questions.explanation: Mani's PE narrates the stories; the Purāṇa Index (PUI) is the terse locational index to the same material.

questions.question: You want the classical *synonyms* of a word (a thesaurus), in the traditional kośa form. Which?
questions.explanation: ABCH (Hemacandra) and Abhidhānaratnamālā (ARMH) are synonym kośas — they group the Sanskrit words for a concept. The big modern kośas are SKD and VCP.

questions.question: You want a name or subject in *Vedic* literature, with scholarly references. Which?
questions.explanation: Macdonell & Keith's Vedic Index of Names and Subjects is the standard scholarly index to Vedic realia.

questions.question: You need a root's grammatical profile — conjugation class, seṭ/aniṭ, pada, and its kṛt-derived forms. Which resource?
questions.answer: Kṛdantarūpamālā (KRM) — a Pāṇinian reference of roots and their kṛt-derived forms (a grammar, not a glossary).

questions.question: You want the *earliest* major Sanskrit-English dictionary, the foundation Monier-Williams built on. Which?
questions.explanation: Wilson (1832) is the first major Sanskrit-English dictionary; MW later built on it.

questions.question: You want to compare how Monier-Williams treated a word in the 1872 first edition vs the 1899 edition. Which two codes?
questions.answer: MW72 (the 1872 first edition) vs MW (the 1899 enlarged edition) — distinct texts.

questions.question: You want a compact, accented student's dictionary to read with — short definitions, accent shown. Which?
questions.explanation: Macdonell's is the compact accented student dictionary; it pairs with his grammars.

questions.question: You found a word/sense missing from Böhtlingk's shorter dictionary (PW) and want the additions a scholar gathered for it. Which?
questions.answer: SCH — Schmidt's Nachträge (1928), the German supplement to PW, keyed to its numbered senses.
